dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for breathing problems at --Address Redacted-- in the city of [CITY].
Hello, my name is Marc, an EMT with the rescue squad. What brought you to call 911?
I've been having shortness of breath for about two hours.
Mr. Harrison, can you tell me your full name?
My name is James Harrison.
Can you tell me where we are right now?
We're in [CITY].
Do you know the date today?
October 9th. Wait, no-it's May 13th.
Can you tell me what made you call the ambulance today?
I started feeling winded and short of breath. It's been like this for a while now.
Mr. Harrison, keep your eyes open while I continue the assessment.
Mr. Harrison, do you understand what we are doing here?
Could you follow my finger with your eyes?
Sure.
Let me check your airway. Can you please sit a bit taller for me?
I can sit up, just give me a second.
Your airway is clear and patent with no signs of blockage.
I am now placing a stethoscope on your chest to assess your breathing. Can you take a few deep breaths for me?
Of course.
Your breathing rate is 38 per minute, which is very fast.
Your oxygen levels are at 78%, which is below the normal range. We need to monitor that closely.
Now, I'll assess your pulse and blood pressure.
I'm ready.
Your pulse is 117 beats per minute, which is elevated.
Your blood pressure is 160 over 95, which is also above normal.
I will now attempt to check your blood sugar level with a small finger prick.
Unfortunately, we couldn't obtain a reliable blood sugar measurement at this time.
Mr. Harrison, how would you describe your shortness of breath?
I can still speak in full sentences, but it is noticeably difficult to breathe.
Has the difficulty been consistent or intermittent?
It's been ongoing for the entire two hours, and it's what prompted the 911 call.
On a scale from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst possible level of discomfort, how would you rate your current state of breathing difficulty?
I've been 0 in terms of pain. Breathing is uncomfortable but not painful.
Might you or the patient have any chronic conditions that could be contributing to the breathing issue?
He has COPD and is on treatment for high blood pressure.
Does the patient also have any history affecting his mental functioning?
Yes, he has schizoaffective disorder.
Mr. Harrison, when was the last time you had anything to eat or drink?
I had some water an hour ago. I haven't felt like food recently.
I detect some wheezing at the lung bases, which is consistent with COPD flare-ups.
Let me conduct an EKG to monitor your heart rhythm more thoroughly. I need to place these electrodes on you.
The four-lead ECG monitor has been applied and is providing consistent readings.
The EKG is showing sinus tachycardia along with occasional extra heartbeats, and there is a left axis deviation, possibly due to lung-related concerns.
I am now preparing to deliver some medication to help improve your breathing.
A nebulizer has been set up with 5 mg of Albuterol and 0.5 mg of Ipratropium to assist with airway opening.
We are treating this according to protocol 3-11 for respiratory distress. Our interventions are in line with the current condition.
Let me reassess your breathing.
Your breathing has improved to 30 breaths per minute, which is a better rate.
Your heart rate is down slightly to 101 beats per minute, which is also a good sign.
Your blood pressure is now at 140 over 104.
Your oxygen levels have improved from 78% to 99%-very close to a normal level.
The EKG is still showing sinus tachycardia and some ventricular premature beats with the left axis deviation still present.
Your breathing is beginning to improve, and the medication from the nebulizer is assisting with that.
We'll be transporting you to [COMPANY_NAME] ER for further care. That works with your preferences, right?
Yes, that's fine.
Since you were able to walk without difficulty, we can safely assist you onto the stretcher.
Yes, I walked fine earlier with no issues.
We will guide you onto the stretcher and make sure you're comfortable while preparing for transport.
We are obtaining a 12-lead ECG now for more comprehensive monitoring before transport.
You are now safely lying on the stretcher, and we're ready to proceed.
I have made sure the hospital is ready to receive you in room 27.
Just to confirm, you are a 62-year-old male?
Yes, that's me.
You have a Glasgow Coma Scale of 15, showing you are fully alert and oriented.
Your condition is stable and improving, and we are ready to move you.
We are now at [COMPANY_NAME] ER and will hand over your care to a nurse in room 27.
The hospital has confirmed they're ready. Let's get you inside now.
